file_name: Milantev et al. - 2020 - Skin lesion analysis using ensemble of CNN with dermoscopic images and metadata.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (EfficientNet-B0-B7, SENet-154, ResNeXt-101 32x4d, and Inception-ResNet-v2) combined with R2U-Net for segmentation, to classify skin lesions from dermoscopic images and patient metadata.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work for the ISIC Skin Lesion Classification Challenge, describing a novel approach using an ensemble of CNNs (EfficientNet, SENet, ResNeXt, Inception-ResNet) and R2U-Net for segmentation, along with specific data preprocessing and metadata integration methods. It reports original results (sensitivity 81.5%, specificity 97.7%) and is published in conference proceedings (Majorov International Conference on Software Engineering and Computer Systems).

title: Skin Lesion Analysis Using Ensemble of CNN with Dermoscopic Images and Metadata
journal: Proceedings of the 12th Majorov International Conference on Software Engineering and Computer Systems
brief_summary: This study addresses the ISIC Skin Lesion Classification Challenge by developing a deep learning approach to classify dermoscopic images and patient metadata into eight lesion classes plus an unknown class. The authors utilized a combined dataset of 32,748 images from the ISIC archive and external sources (SD-198, PH2, MED-NODE, Seven-Point, and Light Field), applying extensive preprocessing including R2U-Net segmentation, black area removal, and metadata imputation. The classification model employed an ensemble of EfficientNet-B0-B7, SENet-154, ResNeXt-101 32x4d, and Inception-ResNet-v2, with metadata integrated via dense layers. Evaluation on the test set demonstrated that the best ensemble achieved a sensitivity of 81.5% and a specificity of 97.7%. The results indicate that advanced data preprocessing and segmentation significantly improve classification accuracy for skin lesion analysis.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (8 skin lesion classes plus an unknown class)
all_task_types.details: The paper addresses the ISIC Skin Lesion Classification Challenge, predicting one of nine discrete classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Unknown) from dermoscopic images and patient metadata. The model uses an ensemble of CNNs (EfficientNet-B0-B7, SENet-154, ResNeXt-101 32x4d, Inception-ResNet-v2) with weighted cross-entropy loss. Evaluation metrics include sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score per class.

R2U-Net was used for image segmentation to remove unnecessary parts (black areas) of the image before feeding them into the classification network. This is a preprocessing step to improve classification accuracy, not the primary prediction task of the final ensemble model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (ISIC Dataset)
datasets_used.description: The main dataset used for the ISIC Skin Lesion Classification Challenge, containing dermoscopic images and patient metadata (age, anatomical site, sex). It aggregates data from HAM10000, BCN20000, and MSK datasets.

use.validation: Included in the 10% train-valid split (part of the 29,473 training images or 3,275 validation images).
use.testing: Used for the ISIC Challenge test set evaluation.

datasets_used.name: SD-198
datasets_used.description: A private clinical skin lesion dataset containing macroscopic images (taken without microscopic zoom) captured with digital cameras and smartphones. Used to enrich the 'Unknown' class.

use.training: Used for training the unknown class.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset

datasets_used.description: A private clinical skin lesion dataset containing dermoscopic images with structured meta information. Two rare classes were used as the unknown class.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to increase dataset size and improve model generalization.
Combined predictions from multiple models (EfficientNet-B0-B7, SENet-154, ResNeXt-101 32x4d, Inception-ResNet-v2) to improve classification performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compared results with state-of-the-art models in a table, but did not perform statistical significance testing between models.

ISIC Skin Lesion Classification Challeng.notes: The best ensemble achieved a sensitivity of 81.5% and specificity of 97.7% on the provided test data.

Per-Class Ensemble Results (Test Set).notes: Table 2 in the paper reports per-class metrics for the ensemble model on the test set.

Comparison with State-of-the-Art Models.notes: Table 1 compares the suggested model's sensitivity with other state-of-the-art models. The paper notes that its approach obtained 'fairly high sensitivity rates' compared to these works.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of EfficientNet-B0-B7, SENet-154, ResNeXt-101 32x4d, and Inception-ResNet-v2 combined with R2U-Net for segmentation

architecture_details.segmentation_model: R2U-Net (Recurrent Residual Convolutional Neural Network based on U-Net with recurrent convolutional layers and residual units)
architecture_details.metadata_integration: Dense neural network with two layers (256 neurons each), batch normalization, ReLU activation, and dropout (0.25); features combined with classification CNN via fully-connected layer (1024 neurons) then classification layer
architecture_details.ensemble_strategy: Averaged predictions from 25 predictions per image based on random transformation strategy

preprocessing: Label encoding for binary features, one-hot encoding for others
Median imputation for missing metadata values

classification_or_output_head.metadata_head_structure: Two-layer dense network (256 neurons/layer) with batch norm, ReLU, dropout (0.25); combined with 1024-neuron layer; final classification layer

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Skin Lesion Classification Using Ensembles of Multi-Resolution EfficientNets with Meta Data
Skin Lesion Classification with Deep Learning Ensembles in ISIC 2020
preprocessing_techniques: Removal of bottom strip containing clinic/image information (SD-198 dataset)
Removal of continuous black areas using a 3% fuzz factor
Color transformation to Shades of Grey (p=6)
R2U-Net segmentation for skin lesion region extraction and removal of unnecessary image parts
Random resizing and scaling of segmented images
Data augmentation: random rotation (-45 to 45 degrees)
Data augmentation: random horizontal/vertical flip or 90-degree rotation
Data augmentation: random brightness and contrast change (<=10%)
Metadata imputation using median values for missing or outlier values
Label encoding for binary metadata features
One-hot encoding for non-binary metadata features
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for metadata dimensionality reduction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a probability of 0.5, rotating between -45 and 45 degrees.
Applied with a probability of 0.5, zooming between 1.0 (original) and 1.12.
Applied with a probability of 0.15, changing no more than 10% from the original.
Applied with a probability of 0.5, using a single cutout of size 16x16.

conclusions: The proposed approach, which combines an ensemble of CNNs (EfficientNet-B0-B7, SENet-154, ResNeXt-101 32x4d, and Inception-ResNet-v2) with R2U-Net segmentation and patient metadata, achieved a sensitivity of 81.5% and specificity of 97.7% on the ISIC Skin Lesion Classification Challenge test set.
Advanced data preprocessing, including R2U-Net segmentation to remove unnecessary parts and black areas, had a positive impact on the accuracy of the skin lesion classification model.
Preprocessing patient metadata (handling missing values with the median method, encoding, and dimensionality reduction) incorporated via dense layers improved the classification results.
The EfficientNet ensemble strategy allowed the model to effectively handle various input resolutions by utilizing models from EfficientNet-B0 (224x224) to EfficientNet-B7 (600x600).

The study highlights that data preprocessing and segmentation are critical components often overlooked in state-of-the-art works, and their consistent application significantly enhances model performance.

The best ensemble outperformed several state-of-the-art models in terms of sensitivity, including Skin Lesion Classification Using Ensembles of Multi-Resolution EfficientNets with Meta Data (74.2%), MelaNet (78.1%), and Skin Lesion Classification with Deep Learning Ensembles in ISIC 2020 (80%).
important_remarks: The study relies heavily on data preprocessing (R2U-Net segmentation, black area removal, metadata imputation) which the authors claim is often overlooked in state-of-the-art works, suggesting this preprocessing is a key driver of performance rather than just architecture.
The dataset includes external private datasets (SD-198, Seven-Point Criteria) that require signing agreements, which may limit reproducibility and public access to the full training data.

The 'Unknown' class was artificially enriched with images from diverse sources (healthy skin, warts, cysts, macroscopic images from SD-198) to emulate natural clinic data, which introduces potential domain shift and label noise compared to standard ISIC classes.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for the comparison with state-of-the-art models, relying instead on point estimates of sensitivity and specificity.

The model was evaluated on the ISIC Challenge test set, but the specific split of the external datasets (SD-198, PH2, etc.) into train/validation/test is not explicitly detailed beyond the 10% split of the merged ISIC data, raising potential concerns about data leakage or non-independent validation if external data overlaps with the test set.

The ensemble strategy involves averaging 25 predictions per image based on random transformations, which increases computational cost and inference time significantly compared to single-model approaches.

Metadata handling used the median method for missing values, which may not be optimal for all feature distributions, and PCA was used for dimensionality reduction without reporting the explained variance or number of components retained.

The study addresses a multi-class classification problem with significant class imbalance, mitigated by weighted cross-entropy loss, but does not provide detailed per-class performance metrics for all classes in the main comparison table (Table 1 only shows sensitivity/specificity for the ensemble, not per-class breakdown).
